text: The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, beginning with the dissection of the greater omentum from the transverse colon, moving from the left to the right side. Dissection continues along the gastroepiploic vessels and station IV lymph nodes. The retroduodenal space is dissected, skeletonizing station V and VI lymph nodes near the suprapyloric and intrapyloric areas. The duodenum is transected distal to the pylorus, and the duodenal stump is oversewn with two OO absorbable sutures. Dissection proceeds along the pars flaccida and station III lymph nodes on the lesser curvature, where the left gastric vessels are identified, dissected from station VII lymph nodes, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon dissects the retrogastric space, skeletonizing station eleven lymph nodes. Dissection continues along the right and left crura, skeletonizing station one and two lymph nodes. The phrenoesophageal fat pad is dissected, and the esophagus is mobilized into the abdominal cavity before being transected. Dissection progresses along the greater curvature of the stomach towards the spleen, detaching posterior and lateral attachments. The splenic hilum is identified, and splenectomy is completed with specimen retrieval. A Roux-en-Y operation is performed, including esophago-jejunostomy with a 25 mm circular stapler, followed by jejuno-jejunostomy between the biliopancreatic limb and common channel. Anchoring sutures are placed on the crura for a tension-free anastomosis.
